meta.title: Physics-Informed Candidate Ranking for Distribution Grid Topology Reconstruction

slides.eyebrow: Final Year Project · AI-based model discovery for active distribution networks
slides.headline: Physics-Informed Candidate Ranking for Distribution Grid Topology Reconstruction
slides.subheadline: Reconstructing low-voltage radial network topology from smart-meter voltage and current measurements.

cards.title: Approach
cards.body: Learned candidate ranking with physics-informed features.

slides.note: Open by defining the project in one sentence: the model ranks complete candidate topologies, rather than predicting edges independently.

slides.headline: Low-voltage networks are becoming harder to monitor accurately.
slides.subheadline: More dynamic behaviour means topology records can become incomplete, outdated, or uncertain.

cards.title: Network change
cards.body: Rooftop solar, EVs, flexible loads, and changing demand patterns make LV networks less predictable.

cards.title: Why topology matters
cards.body: Accurate topology supports state estimation, monitoring, planning, and control.

cards.title: Practical issue
cards.body: Existing LV topology records may not reflect the true current network state.

cards.title: Opportunity
cards.body: Smart-meter data can reveal structure through measured electrical behaviour.

slides.note: Frame this as a real network visibility problem, not just a machine learning exercise.

slides.headline: The gap is not only topology inference, but how complete candidates are selected.
slides.subheadline: Most existing approaches focus on correlations, graphical learning, optimisation, or direct edge prediction.
slides.cta: Research gap: physics-informed candidate ranking for LV topology reconstruction.
slides.note: This is the core novelty slide. Keep it crisp.

slides.headline: The model scores full radial topology candidates and selects the best one.
slides.subheadline: The key idea is to rank entire candidate networks instead of predicting edges independently.

steps.title: Generate candidates
steps.body: Create multiple possible radial topology alternatives.

steps.title: Extract features
steps.body: Use node, source, candidate, and physics-informed features.

steps.title: Score candidates
steps.body: Apply a learned ranking model to each topology.

slides.note: Explain that candidates are complete possible networks, not individual edge guesses.

slides.headline: The ranker combines measurement, source, and topology-level information.
slides.subheadline: Candidate-based features are computed from each proposed topology, not from the unknown real topology.

cards.title: Node features
cards.body: Voltage statistics, real and imaginary current statistics, current magnitude statistics, and observed-node flags.

cards.title: Source features
cards.body: Transformer or source voltage behaviour and source-level measurement statistics.

cards.title: Candidate features
cards.body: Degree statistics, observed-edge ratios, signal-based scores, and radial structure information.

slides.note: Stress that the real topology is not used as an input at inference time.

slides.headline: Separate encoders learn representations, then output one score per candidate.
slides.subheadline: Training teaches the model to rank the true topology above generated alternatives.

steps.title: Encode inputs
steps.body: Separate encoders process node, source, and candidate topology features.

steps.title: Combine signals
steps.body: Encoded representations are fused into a shared candidate representation.

steps.title: Score topology
steps.body: The model outputs one scalar score for each candidate.

steps.title: Learn ranking
steps.body: True topology is positive; generated alternatives are negative.

cards.title: Objective
cards.body: Learn to rank the true topology higher than incorrect candidates.

cards.title: Optimisation
cards.body: Ranking/classification-style loss with Adam-style gradient optimisation.

slides.note: For ML questions: mention scalar score, positive/negative candidates, and ranking objective.

slides.headline: The model strongly narrows the candidate set on previously unseen examples.
slides.subheadline: Evaluation used 108 test examples that were not used during training.
bullets: Model often identifies the exact topology.
True topology is usually ranked very highly.
Top-3 result shows effective candidate narrowing.
slides.note: This is the proof slide. Avoid calling it validation; these are unseen test samples.

slides.headline: Predictions should be statistically likely and electrically plausible.
slides.subheadline: Physics-informed checks aim to improve robustness when candidates are realistic and difficult to distinguish.

cards.title: Voltage consistency
cards.body: Parent-child voltage and source-to-customer voltage behaviour.

cards.title: Feeder trends
cards.body: Radial feeder voltage patterns and plausible downstream behaviour.

cards.title: Current-flow consistency
cards.body: Candidate edges should align with measured electrical signals.

cards.title: Solar challenge
cards.body: Rooftop solar can complicate simple voltage-drop assumptions.

slides.note: Acknowledge that physics is useful, but naive voltage-drop rules can fail with solar.

slides.headline: Learned candidate ranking is a promising path toward smart-meter-based topology discovery.
slides.subheadline: The approach achieved strong unseen-sample performance and provides a clear path for physics-informed improvement.
bullets: Top-3 accuracy reached 98.1% on unseen tests.
Physics can improve realism and electrical plausibility.
Next: realistic candidates and measurement noise testing.
Goal: robust topology discovery from smart-meter data.
slides.cta: Next step: test harder real-world candidate sets.
slides.note: Finish by showing this is already useful as a candidate narrowing tool, with clear next steps.
